text: Let's listen in on the originalist, kicking off the debate over what the law actually means.
That's the Originalist, moments before the panel voted seven to two to reverse the lower court. I'm the court-watcher on this beat.
And I'm your legal-affairs correspondent. This is Split Decision.

Today we are looking at a fight over hazardous waste on a beach in Guam. The United States Air Force blows up old, unusable munitions in the open air at Tarague Beach. This site sits right above the island's only source of drinking water and is a home for endangered sea turtles. The people of Guam rely on this water every single day. The military has been doing this for decades. Every three years, they need a permit from the local Guam environmental agency to keep burning and exploding these materials. When their latest permit was about to expire, the Air Force applied for a new one. But they did not do the massive, broad environmental study that federal law normally demands before taking this kind of action. The local agency accepted the application and let the military keep operating under an automatic extension while they reviewed public comments. A local resident sued, arguing the Air Force broke the law by skipping the big environmental study. The trial court threw the case out, but an appeals court brought it back, siding with the resident.

And the stakes here go way beyond one beach in the Pacific. The federal government is arguing that when they ask a local agency for a permit, that application is just a proposal. It is not a final decision. If the Supreme Court agrees, the military could skip the big environmental reviews across the entire country just by labeling their paperwork a request. It is a classic clash between federal power and local control, with the island's drinking water and public health caught in the middle. If the feds win, they get a blank check to skip the paperwork.
Here is the panel convening and laying out the two main legal questions they need to answer.

So the first question is whether a permit application counts as a final government action that a court can actually review right now. The second question is whether the military had to complete the broad environmental study before even submitting that application to the local agency in the first place.
The civil libertarian jumps in next, arguing that the textualist is using a narrow reading of the law to let the military off the hook.

The civil libertarian is trying to keep this ruling very narrow. They do not want to create a sweeping new rule about every single government application in the country. They just want to hold the Air Force accountable for this specific mess in Guam and leave the bigger theories about administrative finality for another day.
Then we get into the deep weeds of the statute, with the textualist and the living constitutionalist going back and forth over the exact wording of the law.

The textualist is insisting that the law means exactly what it says on the page. An application is just a request. You cannot magically turn a simple request into a final, binding decision just because you dislike the real-world consequences of waiting for the process to finish.
The federalism hawk chimes in here, focusing heavily on the relationship between the federal government and the territory of Guam.

This is the states' rights argument. The federalism hawk is saying the military cannot use its federal power to bully the local environmental agency. If Guam wants to regulate its own environment and require certain reviews, the federal government should not be allowed to ignore those local rules and usurp the local process.
Finally, the civil libertarian casts their vote, cementing the majority position to send the case back.

And that brings us to the final tally. The panel voted seven to two to reverse the lower court and side with the resident. The living constitutionalist and the civil libertarian were the two dissenters in this split, arguing for a different approach to the federal government's duties.

Now, the real Supreme Court has not decided this case yet. So this seven-to-two vote is a public prediction. We are putting it on the record today. If the real Court goes the other way, our panel's score takes a direct hit. And honestly, they desperately need the points to catch up.

Let's look at the season scoreboard to see where they stand. Our solo model is getting the final Supreme Court outcomes right eighty-three percent of the time. The silent jury is at seventy-seven percent. But our deliberating panel? They are sitting at just sixty-six percent.

They are losing, counselor. The deliberation is actually hurting their accuracy this season. When they talk, they drift. They get distracted by grand theories and miss the simple statutory text. It is fascinating to watch the personalities clash, but it is costing them wins against the simpler models.

We will see if they can turn it around as we get closer to the end of the term. Their rulings on all pending cases are on the public record, and we will hold them to it. I'm your legal-affairs correspondent.
And I'm the court-watcher. Thanks for listening to Split Decision.
